name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١١)
```

```english
woe to the deniers on that day (11)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
—those who play around in vain talk, (12)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلَىٰ نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا (١٣)
```

```english
the day when they will be shoved forcibly toward the fire of hell, [and told:] (13)
```

```arabic
هَـٰذِهِ ٱلنَّارُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
‘This is the Fire which you used to deny! (14)
```

```arabic
أَفَسِحْرٌ هَـٰذَآ أَمْ أَنتُمْ لَا تُبْصِرُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
Is this, then, [also] magic, or is it you who do not perceive? (15)
```

```arabic
ٱصْلَوْهَا فَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ أَوْ لَا تَصْبِرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
Enter it, and it will be the same for you whether you are patient or impatient. You are only being requited for what you used to do.’ (16)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ (١٧)
```

```english
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and bliss, (17)
```

```arabic
فَـٰكِهِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٨)
```

```english
rejoicing because of what their Lord has given them, and that their Lord has saved them from the punishment of hell. (18)
```

```arabic
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
[They will be told:] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, [as a reward] for what you used to do.’ (19)
```

```arabic
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّصْفُوفَةٍ ۖ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ (٢٠)
```

```english
They will be reclining on arrayed couches, and We will wed them to big-eyed houris. (20)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَـٰنٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَآ أَلَتْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ (٢١)
```

```english
The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will make their descendants join them, and We will not stint anything from [the reward of] their deeds. Every person is hostage to what he has earned. (21)
```

```arabic
وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰهُم بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
We will provide them with fruits and meat, such as they desire. (22)
```

```arabic
يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ فِيهَا كَأْسًا لَّا لَغْوٌ فِيهَا وَلَا تَأْثِيمٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
There they will pass from hand to hand a cup wherein there will be neither any vain talk nor sinful speech. (23)
```

```arabic
۞ وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ غِلْمَانٌ لَّهُمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَّكْنُونٌ (٢٤)
```

```english
They will be waited upon by their youths, as if they were guarded pearls. (24)
```

```arabic
وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
They will turn to one another, questioning each other. (25)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِىٓ أَهْلِنَا مُشْفِقِينَ (٢٦)
```

```english
They will say, ‘Indeed, aforetime, we used to be apprehensive about our families. (26)
```

```arabic
فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا وَوَقَىٰنَا عَذَابَ ٱلسَّمُومِ (٢٧)
```

```english
But Allah showed us favour and He saved us from the punishment of the [infernal] miasma. (27)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْبَرُّ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٢٨)
```

```english
We used to supplicate Him aforetime. Indeed He is the All-benign, the All-merciful.’ (28)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses mention those upon whom this punishment—the occurrence and realization of which is beyond doubt—shall fall, and describe their state at that time. This is the primary objective of the Surah, as previously indicated. As for the description of the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) — *those who possess godwariness (التقوى), the innate protective shield of the soul against divine displeasure* — on that day, it is mentioned secondarily to reinforce the intended warning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ** — "Woe to the deniers on that day!" (52:11). This is a derivation from what the preceding verses indicated regarding the certainty of the punishment occurring on the Day of Resurrection. That is, if the matter is as mentioned and there is no escape from the occurrence of the punishment, then woe to those upon whom it falls, and they are inevitably the deniers. Thus, the sentence indicates that the punished are the deniers by implication (الاستلزام), and indicates the attachment of "woe" to them by direct correspondence (المطابقة).

Alternatively, the estimation is: if the punishment is inevitably occurring, and it inevitably falls only upon the deniers—because they are the ones who disbelieve in Allah and deny the Day of Resurrection—then woe be to them on that day. Thus, that which indicates the attachment of the punishment to the deniers is His saying: **عَذابَ رَبِّكَ** — "the punishment of your Lord" (52:7), because the punishment of Allah only falls upon those who were called but did not respond and denied His call.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ** — "who play around in [vain] talk" (52:12). Vain talk (الخوض) is entering into falsehood of speech. Al-Raghib said: "Vain talk (الخوض) is originally wading into water and passing through it, and it is used metaphorically for affairs; most of its occurrences in the Qur'an refer to that which is blameworthy to enter into." End quote. The nunnation (التنوين) of indefiniteness in **خَوْضٍ** indicates a deleted adjective, i.e., "in a strange kind of vain talk."

Since engaging in falsehood of speech yields no true result but only an imaginary one embellished by the estimation (الوهم) for the one wading into it, He called it "play" (لعب) — and play (اللعب) consists of those actions that have nothing but an imaginary effect.

The meaning is: those who persist in a strange kind of vain talk, playing around by disputing the signs of Allah, denying them, and mocking them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلى نارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا** — "the day they will be pushed into the fire of hell with a [violent] push" (52:13). Pushing (الدع) is a forceful thrust. It appears that "the day" (يَوْمَ) is an explanation for His saying: "on that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هذِهِ النَّارُ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ بِها تُكَذِّبُونَ** — "[and told,] ‘This is the fire that you used to deny!’" (52:14). That is, it will be said to them: "This is the fire that you used to deny." What is meant by denying the fire is denying what the Prophets (peace be upon them) informed of through revelation from Allah regarding the existence of this fire and that the criminals would be punished by it. The sum of the meaning is: "This is the manifestation (مصداق) of what the Prophets informed you of, which you then denied."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَسِحْرٌ هذا أَمْ أَنْتُمْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ** — "Is this magic, then, or do you not see?" (52:15). This is a derivation from His saying: "This is the fire that you used to deny." The interrogation is for rebuke (الإنكار), derived for them; meaning, if this is that very fire which you used to deny, then this is not magic as you used to accuse the reports of the Prophets of being magic, nor is this an imaginary, superstitious matter as you used to utter, but rather a matter seen and witnessed by you. The verse carries the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِّ** — "The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be asked,] ‘Is this not real?’" (46:34).

From the aforementioned meaning, it becomes clear that "or" (أَمْ) in His saying: **أَمْ أَنْتُمْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ** is conjunctive (متصلة). It has been said it is disjunctive (منقطعة), but this is not without far-fetchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اصْلَوْها فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "Enter it! Then be patient or impatient; it is the same for you. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (52:16). Entering (الصلي) — with a *fathah* then a *sukun* — is to endure the heat of the fire. Thus, the meaning of "Enter it" is: endure the heat of the fire of Hell.

His saying: **فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا** — "Then be patient or impatient" — is a derivation from the command to endure. The alternation between the command and the prohibition is a metaphor for the equality of the action and its abandonment. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: **سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ** — "it is the same for you" — meaning: this endurance is necessary for you and will not leave you, whether you are patient or not. Neither will patience remove the punishment from you or lighten it, nor will anguish and the lack of patience benefit you in any way.

His saying: **سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ** is the predicate (خبر) of a deleted subject (مبتدأ), i.e., "they both are equal." The use of the singular **سَواءٌ** is because it is originally a verbal noun (مصدر).

His saying: **إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "You are only being requited for what you used to do" — is in the position of providing the rationale (التعليل) for what was mentioned regarding the necessity of the punishment and the equality of patience and anguish.

The meaning is: this evil requital is necessary for you and will not leave you because you are being requited by your very deeds that you used to do, and the relationship of the deed to its doer cannot be severed; thus, the punishment is inseparable from you. Or, it means you are only being requited by the consequences and the recompense of what you used to do.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ** — "Indeed the God-conscious will be in gardens and bliss" (52:17). The garden (الجنة) is the orchard whose trees cover and shade it, and bliss (النعيم) is abundant favor (النعمة). That is, those characterized by godwariness (تقوى) of Allah shall be, on that day, in gardens where they reside and surrounded by abundant favor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فاكِهِينَ بِما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ** — "rejoicing in what their Lord has given them, and their Lord has saved them from the punishment of hell" (52:18). Fruit (الفاكهة) refers to fruit in general. It has been said it refers to fruit other than grapes and pomegranates. It is said *tafakkaha* and *fakiha* when one engages in jesting (الفكاهة), and *tafakkaha* and *fakiha* when one consumes fruit (الفاكهة). The verse has been interpreted with both meanings. It was said the meaning is: they converse about the bliss their Lord has given them. It was said the meaning is: they consume the fruits and produce their Lord has given them. It was said the meaning is: they take pleasure in the benevolence of their Lord, which returns to the first meaning. It was said it means they are joyful and delighted (فاكهين) with what their Lord has given them, which perhaps returns to the second meaning.

The repetition of "their Lord" (رَبُّهُمْ) in His saying: **وَوَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ** is to indicate increased care (العناية) for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "[They will be told,] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, as a reward for what you used to do’" (52:19). That is, it will be said to them: "Eat and drink a wholesome eating and drinking," or "wholesome food and drink." Thus, "wholesome" (هَنِيئاً) is an adjective standing in the place of an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) or a direct object (مفعول به).

His saying: **بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "for what you used to do" — is connected to His saying: "Eat and drink," or to His saying: "wholesome."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى سُرُرٍ مَصْفُوفَةٍ وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ** — "reclining on couches ranged in rows, and We shall wed them to big-eyed houris" (52:20). Reclining (الاتكاء) is leaning upon a pillow or the like. Couches (السرر) is the plural of *sarir*. "Ranged in rows" (مصفوفة) is from *saff*, meaning lined up and joined one to another. The meaning is: reclining on pillows and cushions, sitting upon couches arranged in rows.

His saying: **وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ** — "and We shall wed them to big-eyed houris." What is meant by "wedding" (التزويج) is pairing (القرن), meaning We have paired them with them, rather than marriage by contract (النكاح بالعقد). The evidence for this is its transitivity with the particle *ba* (ب), for "wedding" in the sense of marriage by contract is transitive by itself, as the Exalted said: **زَوَّجْناكَها** — "We wedded her to you" (33:37). So it has been said.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" (52:21). It has been said that the difference between "following" (الاتباع) and "joining" (اللحوق)—while considering precedence and subsequence in both—is that in "following," a commonality is considered between the follower and the followed in the matter of following, unlike "joining," where the joiner does not necessarily share with the joined that which he joined him in.

*Lata* (لات) and *alata* (ألات) mean to diminish (نقص). Thus, the meaning of "We did not stint them" (ما ألتناهم) is: We did not diminish anything from their deeds by the act of joining.

The apparent sense of the verse is that it is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان). Thus, He, the Glorified, bestows favor upon those who believe that He will join with them their descendants who followed them in faith, so that their eyes may be comforted thereby. This is the context clue (القرينة) that the nunnation in "in faith" (بِإِيمانٍ) is for indefiniteness (التنكير) rather than for magnification (التعظيم).

The meaning is: they followed them with some degree of faith, even if it fell short of the level of their fathers' faith; for there would be no special favor bestowed if their faith were more perfect than their fathers' faith or equal to it.

The absolute use of "following" in faith is understood as the following of one from whom faith is valid in itself, by having reached the stage of being religiously accountable (التكليف). Thus, what is meant by "descendants" (الذرية) are the adult children who are accountable for faith. Therefore, the verse does not include small children who died before reaching maturity, and this does not contradict the fact that the small children of believers are legally ruled to be believers.

Unless, however, generality is derived from the indefiniteness of "faith," and the meaning becomes: "and their descendants followed them with some faith," whether it was faith in itself or faith according to the legal ruling.

Similarly, the bestowing of favor is a context clue that the pronoun in His saying: **وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" — refers to those who believe, just like the two pronouns in His saying: **وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ** — "and their descendants followed them." This is because His saying: "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" is then intended to repel the illusion that a deficiency in reward would occur upon the decree of joining, which would contradict the bestowing of favor. It is well known that what contradicts the favor is the deficiency in the reward of the fathers to whom others are joined, not the descendants.

In summary, His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** etc., is a new sentence (استئناف) in which the Exalted bestows favor upon those who believe, stating that He will join with them their children who followed them with some degree of faith, even if it was short of the level of their faith, so that their eyes may be comforted. Despite this, nothing of the reward of the fathers' deeds is diminished by the joining; rather, He gives them the like of what He gave the fathers, or in a manner where there is no crowding, according to what He knows best.

Regarding the meaning of the verse, there are other opinions not free from absurdity, such as the saying of some that His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** is coordinated (معطوف) with "big-eyed houris," meaning: "We wedded them to big-eyed houris and to those who believe," so they enjoy the houris through marriage and the believers through companionship and society. And the saying of some that what is meant by "descendants" is small children only. And the saying of some that the two pronouns in **وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** refer to the descendants, meaning: "We did not diminish the descendants anything of their deeds because of their being joined to their fathers; rather, We fulfill their deeds for them, whether good or evil, then We join them to their fathers."

His saying: **كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — "Every man is a hostage to what he has earned" (52:21). This is a rationale (تعليل) for His saying: "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds," according to what the context indicates. A pledge (الرهن), a hostage (الرهين), and a pledged object (المرهون) are what is placed as security for a debt, as mentioned by Al-Raghib. He said: "Since a pledge is conceived as being detained, it was used metaphorically for the detention of anything whatsoever." End quote.

Perhaps this metaphorical meaning is what is intended in the verse: a person is a pledge, held and preserved with Allah, the Glorified, for what he has earned of good or evil, until He fulfills for him the recompense of what he did, whether reward or punishment. If He were to diminish anything of his deed and not fulfill it for him, he would not be a hostage to what he earned, but rather a hostage to some of what he did, while another—such as his descendants joined to him—possessed the other part.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ إِلَّا أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ** — "Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned, except the People of the Right" (74:38-39), what is meant is its being a hostage to punishment on the Day of Resurrection, as witnessed by the context of what follows it in His saying: **فِي جَنَّاتٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** — "in gardens, questioning the guilty" (74:40-41).

It has been said: what is meant is that a person is a hostage to his evil deed, as indicated by the verse of Surah al-Muddaththir mentioned just now, by the evidence of the exception of the "People of the Right." Thus, the verse—meaning His saying: **كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — is a parenthetical sentence (جملة معترضة) describing the attributes of the people of the Fire, interposed within the attributes of the people of Paradise.

The author of *al-Kashshaf* interpreted the verse as a type of metaphor, thereby removing the contradiction between the two verses. He said: "It is as if the soul of the servant is a pledge with Allah for the righteous deed he is demanded to perform, just as a man pledges his slave for a debt he owes. If he performs a righteous deed, he ransoms and frees it; otherwise, he destroys it." End quote.

You are well aware that what he mentioned alone does not justify the connection of the sentence—meaning His saying: "Every man is a hostage to what he has earned"—to what precedes it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمْدَدْناهُمْ بِفاكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ** — "We will provide them with such fruits and meat as they desire" (52:22). This is an explanation of some of their completions and enjoyments in Paradise, which were mentioned summarily in His previous saying: "Enjoy your food and drink," etc.

Providing (الإمداد) is to bring a thing time after time. It is used for good, just as *madd* (المد) is used for evil. The Exalted said: **وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا** — "and We will prolong the punishment for him" (19:79).

The meaning is: We provide them with fruit and the meat they desire, a provision after provision and time after time, without interruption.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَتَنازَعُونَ فِيها كَأْساً لا لَغْوٌ فِيها وَلا تَأْثِيمٌ** — "There they will pass to one another a cup wherein is neither vain talk nor sin" (52:23). Contending (التنازع) for the cup is passing it to one another and gathering to consume it. A cup (الكأس) is a goblet, and the term "cup" is only applied when it contains a drink.

What is meant by "vain talk" (اللغو) is the vanity of speech that issues from drinkers of wine in the world. "Sinning" (التأثيم) is making a person a possessor of sin, which is also among the effects of wine in the world. The negation of vain talk and sinning is the context clue that what is meant by the cup they pass to one another is a cup of wine.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ غِلْمانٌ لَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَكْنُونٌ** — "They will be waited upon by their youths, as if they were hidden pearls" (52:24). What is meant is their going around them for service. Some have said: it was said "youths for them" (غِلْمانٌ لَهُمْ) in the indefinite form, and He did not say "their youths" (غلمانهم), lest it be imagined that what is meant by them are their servants who used to serve them in the world. Thus, they are like the houris, among the creations of Paradise, as if they were hidden pearls—stored away—in beauty, radiance, and purity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ** — "They will turn to one another, questioning each other" (52:25). That is, each of them asks the other about his state in the world and what it was that led him to Paradise and bliss.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ** — "They will say, ‘Before this, we used to be apprehensive [of Allah’s punishment] among our folk’" (52:26). Al-Raghib said: "Apprehension (الإشفاق) is a care (عناية) mixed with fear, because the apprehensive one loves the one he is apprehensive for and fears what might befall him. The Exalted said: **وَهُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ** — 'and they are apprehensive of the Hour' (21:49). When it is made transitive with *min* (من), the meaning of fear in it is more apparent, and when it is made transitive with *fi* (في), the meaning of care in it is more apparent. The Exalted said: **إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ**." End quote.

The meaning is: We were in the world possessors of apprehension among our families, caring for their happiness and their salvation from the destruction of misguidance; so we consorted with them with beautiful conduct and walked among them by spreading sincere advice and the call to the truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا وَوَقانا عَذابَ السَّمُومِ** — "So Allah was gracious to us and He saved us from the punishment of the [scorching] Samum" (52:27). Bestowing favor (المن), according to what Al-Raghib mentioned, is to grant a heavy favor (النعمة الثقيلة). It can be by action, which is good, or by speech, which is ugly from other than Allah, the Exalted. The Exalted said: **يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا قُلْ لا تَمُنُّوا عَلَيَّ إِسْلامَكُمْ بَلِ اللهُ يَمُنُّ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْ هَداكُمْ لِلْإِيمانِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — "They count it as a favor to you that they have embraced Islam. Say, ‘Do not count your embracing of Islam as a favor to me. Rather it is Allah who has done you a favor by guiding you to faith, if you are truthful’" (49:17).

The favor from Him, the Exalted, upon the people of Paradise is His granting them the success to enter it through mercy, and its completion is by protecting them from the punishment of the *Samum*.

The *Samum* (السموم) — according to what Al-Tabarsi mentioned — is the heat that enters the pores of the body, causing pain; from it comes the *Samum* wind.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا كُنَّا مِنْ قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْبَرُّ الرَّحِيمُ** — "Before this we used to supplicate Him. Indeed, He is the All-benign, the All-merciful" (52:28). This is a rationale (تعليل) for His saying: "So Allah was gracious to us," etc., just as His saying: "Indeed, He is the All-benign, the All-merciful" is a rationale for it.

This verse, along with the two verses before it, indicates that these people used to call upon Allah in the world by affirming His monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — for worship and submission to His command, and they were apprehensive among their families, bringing them closer to the truth and distancing them from falsehood. This was the cause for Allah bestowing favor upon them with Paradise and protecting them from the punishment of the *Samum*. This was a cause for that only because He, the Exalted, is All-benign and All-merciful, so He acts benevolently toward whoever calls upon Him and has mercy upon him.

Thus, the three verses carry the same meaning as His saying: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَتَواصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ** — "Indeed man is in loss, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth, and enjoin one another to patience" (103:2-3).

The All-benign (البر) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah, and it is from *al-birr* (البر) meaning benevolence (الإحسان). Some have interpreted it as the Subtle (اللطيف).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Bakr, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ** — "The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will unite their descendants with them." He said: "The children fell short of the deeds of the fathers, so the children were joined to the fathers so that their eyes might be comforted thereby."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Tawhid* with its chain of narration to Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from him (peace be upon him).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: My father narrated to me from Sulayman al-Daylami, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, the infants of our Shi‘a among the believers are raised by Fatimah (peace be upon her)." Regarding His saying: **أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ**, he said: "They are presented to their fathers on the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Majma‘* at the end of the hadith from him (peace be upon him) as a disconnected (مرسلا) report.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "When an infant among the infants of the believers dies, a caller cries out in the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: 'Behold, so-and-so son of so-and-so has died.' If his parents or one of them, or some of his family members from the believers have already died, he is handed over to them to nourish him. Otherwise, he is handed over to Fatimah to nourish him until his parents or one of them, or some of his family members from the believers arrive, then she hands him over to them."

In *al-Faqih*: In a narration of al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from ‘Ali, from al-Halabi, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, entrusted Ibrahim and Sarah with the infants of the believers; they nourish them from a tree in Paradise that has teats like the teats of cows in a palace of pearl. When it is the Day of Resurrection, they are clothed, perfumed, and presented to their fathers; so they are kings in Paradise with their fathers. This is the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ**."

In *al-Majma‘*, Zadhan narrated from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "Indeed, the believers and their children are in Paradise," then he recited this verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bazzar and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn ‘Abbas, who attributed it to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), saying: "Indeed, Allah raises the descendants of the believer to him in his rank, even if they were below him in deeds," then he recited: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ**. He said: "And We did not diminish the fathers because of what We gave the children."

In the same source, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn ‘Abbas that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "When a man enters Paradise, he asks about his parents, his descendants, and his children. It is said: 'They did not reach your rank or your deeds.' He says: 'O Lord, I worked for myself and for them.' So it is ordered that they be joined to him." And Ibn ‘Abbas recited: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ** the verse.

**I say:** The verse does not include the "parents" mentioned in the hadith. More suitable to indicate that is what the Exalted mentioned in the supplication of the angels: **رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ وَمَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَأَزْواجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ** — "Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses, and their descendants" (40:8).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying: **لا لَغْوٌ فِيها وَلا تَأْثِيمٌ**, he said: "There is no singing or obscenity in Paradise, and the believer drinks and does not sin." Regarding **وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ**, he said: "In Paradise."
